chapter_summary:
The Stixrude-Lithgow-Bertelloni (SLB2011) formulation is a self-consistent thermodynamic framework that computes all equilibrium thermodynamic and elastic properties of mantle minerals from a minimal set of experimentally determined parameters. The formulation combines Eulerian finite strain theory with the Mie-Gruneisen-Debye thermal model to construct the Helmholtz free energy F(V,T) as a sum of three contributions: a reference energy F0, a cold compression energy F_cold from the third-order Birch-Murnaghan equation of state, and a thermal energy F_thermal from the Debye model. All other properties -- pressure, bulk modulus, shear modulus, thermal expansivity, heat capacity, entropy, Gibbs free energy, and seismic velocities -- are obtained as thermodynamically consistent derivatives of this single free energy function. The key innovation is that the Gruneisen parameter, Debye temperature, and shear modulus are all expressed as explicit functions of finite strain, ensuring internal consistency across all derived properties.

Each mineral endmember in the SLB2011 database is characterized by 11 independent parameters measured or fitted at ambient conditions: reference molar volume V0, isothermal bulk modulus K0, its pressure derivative K'0, shear modulus G0, its pressure derivative G'0, Debye temperature theta0, Gruneisen parameter gamma0, the logarithmic volume derivative of gamma q0, the shear strain derivative of gamma eta_S0, the number of atoms per formula unit n, and the molar mass M. These 11 parameters are stored in the MineralParams class of the ThermoElasticCalculator codebase. From these, derived quantities such as the finite strain coefficients (Aii, Aiikk, As), the cold-compression Birch-Murnaghan expressions for pressure P(f), isothermal bulk modulus K_T(f), and shear modulus G(f) are computed algebraically. The thermal state is determined by the Debye function D3(theta/T), which provides internal energy, heat capacity, entropy, and free energy contributions per atom.

The computational workflow proceeds as follows: given a target pressure P and temperature T, the MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer iteratively finds the finite strain f such that the total pressure P_cold(f) + Delta_P_thermal(f,T) equals the target P. This is a self-consistency requirement because the thermal pressure itself depends on the Gruneisen parameter and Debye temperature, which in turn depend on f. Once the converged f is found, the ThermoMineralParams constructor computes all state variables in a single pass: the compression-dependent Gruneisen parameter gamma(f), Debye temperature theta(f), shear parameter eta_S(f), thermal energy Delta_E, heat capacity Cv, isothermal bulk modulus K_T, thermal expansivity alpha, adiabatic bulk modulus K_S, shear modulus G, density rho, and seismic velocities Vp, Vs, and Vb. The Helmholtz free energy F and Gibbs free energy G = F + PV are also computed for phase equilibrium calculations.

The formulation can be extended beyond the basic 11 parameters to include Landau theory for displacive phase transitions (adding parameters Tc0, VD, SD for the critical temperature, excess volume, and excess entropy), magnetic contributions for transition-metal-bearing minerals (adding spin quantum number S and magnetic atom count r), and reference Helmholtz energy F0 for absolute free energy calculations. These extensions are implemented as additive corrections to volume, entropy, and free energy in the ThermoMineralParams class, preserving the thermodynamic self-consistency of the core SLB framework. The SLB2011 database provides parameters for over 60 endmember minerals spanning the major mantle phases from olivine through post-perovskite, making it the most widely used thermodynamic database for mantle mineralogy.
prerequisite_knowledge: Thermodynamic potentials: Helmholtz free energy F(V,T), Gibbs free energy G(P,T), and their partial derivative relationships
Eulerian finite strain theory and the Birch-Murnaghan equation of state (Ch01)
Debye model of lattice vibrations: Debye function D3(x), internal energy, heat capacity, and entropy (Ch02)
Gruneisen parameter: definition, physical meaning, volume dependence via q0 (Ch03)
Elastic moduli (K, G) and their relationship to seismic velocities (Ch04)
Mie-Gruneisen thermal pressure and its role in thermal equations of state
Basic numerical methods: root-finding algorithms for nonlinear equations (Regula Falsi, bisection)
Crystal chemistry: formula units, atoms per formula unit, molar volume and mass conventions

key_equations.name: Total Helmholtz Free Energy Decomposition

variables.F_0: Reference Helmholtz free energy at standard state [kJ/mol]
variables.F_{\\text{cold}}(f): Cold compression (strain) energy from Birch-Murnaghan EOS [kJ/mol]

key_equations.interpretation: This is the master equation of the SLB formulation. All thermodynamic properties are derived as partial derivatives of this single function. The decomposition into cold and thermal parts ensures that the zero-temperature compression curve is described exactly by the Birch-Murnaghan EOS, while thermal effects are added self-consistently via the Debye model. In ThermoMineralParams, F_cold is the FCold property and F_thermal is the FThermal property.

key_equations.name: Cold Compression Energy (3rd-order Birch-Murnaghan)

variables.K'_0: Pressure derivative of bulk modulus at zero pressure

key_equations.interpretation: The strain energy stored by isothermal compression. The coefficient 9*K0*V0 sets the energy scale, f^2/2 gives the harmonic contribution, and the f^3 term captures anharmonic stiffening controlled by K'0. Since GPa * cm^3/mol = kJ/mol, units are directly compatible. Implemented as ThermoMineralParams.FCold.

key_equations.equation: F_{\text{thermal}} = n \left[ k_B T \cdot g(\theta/T) - k_B T_{\text{ref}} \cdot g(\theta_0/T_{\text{ref}}) \right] N_A / 1000
key_equations.name: Thermal Free Energy from Debye Model

variables.n: Number of atoms per formula unit
variables.g(x): Dimensionless thermal free energy function: 3 ln(1 - e^{-x}) - D_3(x)

key_equations.interpretation: The Debye model thermal free energy, referenced to the standard state at T_ref. The function g(x) combines the zero-point energy and thermal occupation terms. The strain dependence enters through theta(f), coupling compression to thermal properties. Implemented in ThermoMineralParams.FThermal via DebyeFunctionCalculator.GetThermalFreeEnergyPerAtom.

key_equations.name: Total Pressure: Cold + Thermal (Mie-Gruneisen)
key_equations.interpretation: Total pressure is the sum of cold (Birch-Murnaghan) pressure and Mie-Gruneisen thermal pressure. In ThermoMineralParams, RefP gives the cold part via MineralParams.GetPressure(f), and DeltaP = (gamma/V) * DeltaE / 1000 gives the thermal part. The MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer iterates to find the strain f that reproduces the target pressure.

key_equations.name: Strain Polynomial for Debye Temperature Scaling

variables.\\mu: Dimensionless scaling factor for Debye temperature

key_equations.interpretation: The Debye temperature at finite strain is theta(f) = theta_0 * sqrt(mu(f)). The coefficients Aii and Aiikk encode how the vibrational frequency spectrum shifts under compression via gamma_0 and q_0. This is the central coupling between the equation of state and the thermal model. Computed directly in the ThermoMineralParams constructor.

key_equations.name: Gruneisen Parameter at Finite Strain
key_equations.interpretation: The Gruneisen parameter at arbitrary compression, derived from gamma = -d(ln theta)/d(ln V) applied to the mu(f) polynomial. At f=0 (ambient conditions), this reduces to gamma_0. At high compression, gamma generally decreases. Implemented as the _gamma field in ThermoMineralParams.

key_equations.name: Shear Strain Derivative of Gruneisen Parameter
key_equations.interpretation: eta_S describes how the vibrational frequencies shift under shear strain. It controls the thermal correction to the shear modulus. A larger eta_S means stronger temperature sensitivity of G. At f=0, eta_S = eta_S0. Stored as _ethaS in ThermoMineralParams.

key_equations.name: Isothermal Bulk Modulus with Thermal Corrections

variables.\\Delta(C_v T): CvT at T minus CvT at T_ref [J/mol]

key_equations.interpretation: The full expression for K_T at arbitrary P-T. The cold term dominates at high pressure; the two thermal corrections partially cancel. Implemented in ThermoMineralParams.KT property.

key_equations.name: Adiabatic Bulk Modulus
key_equations.interpretation: The adiabatic correction converts K_T (relevant for slow isothermal compression) to K_S (relevant for fast adiabatic seismic waves). The correction gamma^2 * CvT / V / 1000 is always positive, so K_S > K_T. In ThermoMineralParams, CvT = Cv * T is precomputed for efficiency.

key_equations.name: Shear Modulus with Thermal Correction

variables.G^{\\text{cold}}(f): Shear modulus from finite strain expansion (BM3GT) [GPa]

key_equations.interpretation: The shear modulus thermal correction is simpler than for K_T because shear deformation does not change volume. Only the eta_S * DeltaE / V term appears, and since eta_S is positive and DeltaE is positive above the reference temperature, G always decreases with increasing T. Implemented as ThermoMineralParams.GS.

key_equations.name: Volume Derivative of Gruneisen Parameter at Finite Strain

variables.q: Logarithmic volume derivative of gamma at current strain

key_equations.interpretation: q = d(ln gamma)/d(ln V) evaluated at the current compression state. At f=0, q = q_0. This quantity enters the K_T thermal correction and describes how the mode of thermal pressure generation changes with compression. Implemented as ThermoMineralParams.Q.

physical_concepts.definition: A framework in which all thermodynamic properties are derived as partial derivatives of a single free energy function, ensuring that Maxwell relations and thermodynamic identities are automatically satisfied.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Just as a topographic map determines all gradients and contour lines from a single elevation function, the Helmholtz free energy F(V,T) determines pressure (-dF/dV), entropy (-dF/dT), bulk modulus (V * d^2F/dV^2), and all other properties as derivatives. If we used separate ad hoc fits for each property, they would generally be mutually inconsistent.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: A common mistake is to fit K(P,T), alpha(P,T), and Cp(P,T) independently and combine them. This violates Maxwell relations and can produce thermodynamically impossible results such as negative heat capacity or imaginary velocities at extreme conditions.

physical_concepts.definition: The dimensionless strain measure f = [(V0/V)^{2/3} - 1] / 2 that describes volumetric compression relative to the reference state.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Finite strain f is zero at ambient volume and increases monotonically with compression. It provides a natural expansion variable for the free energy because the elastic energy scales smoothly with f. At 25 GPa (upper mantle), f is about 0.02-0.04; at 136 GPa (core-mantle boundary), f reaches 0.08-0.12 for bridgmanite.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: f is not the same as V0/V or fractional volume change. The (V0/V)^{2/3} power arises from the Eulerian strain definition that refers to the deformed configuration. The Lagrangian alternative (referring to the undeformed state) converges more slowly.

physical_concepts.definition: V0, K0, K'0, G0, G'0, theta0, gamma0, q0, eta_S0, n, and M -- the minimal set of experimentally constrained quantities that fully specify a mineral's thermodynamic and elastic behavior in the SLB framework.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: These divide into three groups. The mechanical parameters (V0, K0, K'0, G0, G'0) describe how the crystal resists compression and shear. The thermal parameters (theta0, gamma0, q0, eta_S0) describe how vibrational frequencies and their mode-averaged properties change with volume. The formula parameters (n, M) define the chemical identity. Together they encode everything needed to predict behavior from 0 to 150 GPa and 300 to 5000 K.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: K'0 and G'0 are not second derivatives of K and G. They are first derivatives with respect to pressure (dK/dP and dG/dP at P=0). Also, eta_S0 is not directly measurable from volume compression -- it requires shear wave velocity data at high temperature.

physical_concepts.definition: The thermal contribution to pressure arising from the change in vibrational energy with volume: Delta_P = (gamma/V) * Delta_E_thermal.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: When a crystal is heated, atoms vibrate more vigorously and push their neighbors apart, generating additional pressure. The Gruneisen parameter gamma tells us how efficiently vibrational energy converts to pressure. At mantle conditions, the thermal pressure is typically 5-30 GPa, comparable to the cold pressure.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: The thermal pressure is not simply alpha*K_T*Delta_T. That linear approximation fails at high temperatures and pressures. The Mie-Gruneisen form using the Debye internal energy is valid over a much wider range because it correctly captures the saturation of heat capacity at high T.

physical_concepts.definition: The Debye temperature theta at compression state f is theta(f) = theta_0 * sqrt(mu(f)), where mu(f) is a polynomial in f determined by gamma_0 and q_0.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Compression stiffens the crystal lattice, shifting all vibrational frequencies upward. The Debye temperature characterizes the frequency scale of the vibration spectrum. Under compression to lower mantle conditions, theta can increase by 50-100% from its ambient value.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: The Debye model assumes a single characteristic frequency for all vibrations. Real crystals have complex phonon spectra. Nevertheless, the Debye model works remarkably well for thermodynamic properties (which are frequency-averaged integrals) even when it fails for individual phonon branches.

physical_concepts.definition: The numerical procedure (MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer) that finds the finite strain f such that the computed total pressure P(f,T) equals the target pressure, accounting for the interdependence of thermal and compressive effects.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: The thermal pressure depends on volume (through gamma and theta), but volume depends on total pressure. This circular dependency is resolved by iteration: guess a reference pressure, solve for f, compute thermal pressure, check if the total matches the target, and adjust. Convergence is typically achieved in 3-10 iterations to better than 0.01 mPa accuracy.
physical_concepts.common_misconceptions: It is tempting to simply add thermal pressure to the cold-compression curve without iterating. This 'one-shot' approach can introduce errors of several GPa at high temperatures, leading to significant errors in derived properties like velocities.

key_references.author: Stixrude, L. and Lithgow-Bertelloni, C.
key_references.title: Thermodynamics of mantle minerals - II. Phase equilibria
key_references.why_essential: The definitive reference for the SLB2011 database. Provides the complete mathematical formulation, all endmember parameters (Table A1), and the solid solution mixing models. This is the primary source for the ThermoElasticCalculator implementation.

key_references.author: Stixrude, L. and Lithgow-Bertelloni, C.
key_references.title: Thermodynamics of mantle minerals - I. Physical properties
key_references.why_essential: The companion paper to SLB2011 that lays out the theoretical framework in detail: finite strain expansion, Debye model coupling, and the derivation of all thermodynamic properties from the Helmholtz free energy. Essential for understanding the mathematical structure.

key_references.author: Birch, F.
key_references.title: Finite strain isotherm and velocities for single-crystal and polycrystalline NaCl at high pressures and 300 K
key_references.why_essential: Established the finite strain framework for elastic properties that SLB extended. Shows how K and G can be expressed as polynomials in Eulerian finite strain, the basis for BM3KT and BM3GT.

key_references.author: Cottaar, S., Heister, T., Rose, I., and Unterborn, C.
key_references.title: BurnMan: A lower mantle mineral physics toolkit
key_references.why_essential: The open-source Python implementation of the SLB2011 formulation that serves as the validation reference for ThermoElasticCalculator. BurnMan's SLB2011 module is the community-standard implementation.

key_references.author: Anderson, O.L.
key_references.title: Equations of State of Solids for Geophysics and Ceramic Science
key_references.why_essential: Comprehensive monograph on thermal equations of state. Provides the physical background for the Mie-Gruneisen model, Debye theory, and the relationships between thermodynamic quantities used throughout the SLB formulation.

worked_examples.problem_statement: Compute the density, adiabatic bulk modulus K_S, shear modulus G, and seismic velocities Vp and Vs for forsterite (Mg2SiO4) at P = 14 GPa and T = 1673 K (conditions near the 410 km discontinuity).
worked_examples.solution_approach:
1. Load forsterite parameters from SLB2011Endmembers: V0=43.603 cm^3/mol, K0=127.96 GPa, K'0=4.218, G0=81.6 GPa, G'0=1.4626, theta0=809.17 K, gamma0=0.99282, q0=2.10672, eta_S0=2.2997, n=7, M=140.69 g/mol.
2. Create MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer with P=14 GPa, T=1673 K.
3. The optimizer iterates: (a) estimate reference pressure, (b) compute finite strain f from BM3Finite, (c) construct ThermoMineralParams(f, T, mineral), (d) check if computed total pressure matches 14 GPa, (e) adjust and repeat.
4. After convergence (~5 iterations), read off the properties from ThermoMineralParams.
worked_examples.interpretation: At 410 km conditions, forsterite is significantly stiffer than at ambient (K_S nearly 50% higher than ambient K0). The Gruneisen parameter has decreased from 0.99 to about 0.88, reflecting the reduced anharmonicity under compression. The computed Vp and Vs can be compared with PREM values at 410 km (~9.0 km/s and ~5.1 km/s), showing reasonable agreement for a single-phase endmember. [uncertain]

worked_examples.problem_statement: Demonstrate the self-consistency of the SLB formulation by verifying the thermodynamic identity K_S = K_T * (1 + alpha * gamma * T) for Mg-perovskite (bridgmanite) at P = 100 GPa, T = 2500 K.
worked_examples.solution_approach:
1. Load Mg-Perovskite parameters: V0=24.445, K0=250.53, K'0=4.14, G0=172.9, G'0=1.6904, theta0=905.94, gamma0=1.56508, q0=1.10945, eta_S0=2.5654, n=5, M=100.39.
2. Run MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer at P=100 GPa, T=2500 K.
3. Read off K_T, K_S, alpha, gamma from the converged ThermoMineralParams.
4. Compute K_T * (1 + alpha * gamma * T) and compare with K_S from the direct calculation.
5. The two values should agree to numerical precision, confirming self-consistency.

numerical_values.relative_discrepancy: The identity K_S = K_T + gamma^2*Cv*T/V is the exact form used in the code. The approximate identity K_S = K_T*(1+alpha*gamma*T) holds to within 1-2% due to the approximation Cv ≈ Cp at high compression.

worked_examples.interpretation: The small discrepancy between the two forms of the adiabatic correction arises because K_S = K_T + gamma^2*CvT/V is the exact thermodynamic identity used in ThermoMineralParams.KS, while K_S = K_T*(1+alpha*gamma*T) is an approximation valid when Cv ≈ nR (Dulong-Petit limit). At 2500 K the Debye model predicts Cv slightly below 3nR, causing a ~1% discrepancy. This illustrates why the code uses the exact form. [uncertain]

worked_examples.problem_statement: Trace the complete calculation chain for the Helmholtz free energy, Gibbs free energy, and entropy of Mg-Wadsleyite at P = 16 GPa, T = 1600 K, verifying that G = F + PV and S = -dF/dT.
worked_examples.solution_approach:
1. Load Mg-Wadsleyite parameters: V0=40.515, K0=168.69, K'0=4.3229, G0=112.0, G'0=1.4442, theta0=843.5, gamma0=1.2061, q0=2.0188, eta_S0=2.6368, n=7, M=140.69.
2. Run optimizer at P=16 GPa, T=1600 K to get converged ThermoMineralParams.
3. Compute F = F0 + F_cold + F_thermal from the three contributions.
4. Verify G = F + P*V (GPa * cm^3/mol = kJ/mol).
5. Compute entropy numerically: S = -(F(T+0.5) - F(T-0.5)) / 1.0 * 1000 [J/mol/K], matching the ThermoMineralParams.Entropy implementation.
worked_examples.interpretation: The Gibbs free energy G = F + PV is the thermodynamic potential minimized at fixed P and T, making it the key quantity for phase equilibrium calculations (e.g., determining the olivine-wadsleyite transition at 410 km). The entropy of ~230 J/mol/K at 1600 K is dominated by the Debye thermal contribution (vibrational entropy). The numerical derivative method for entropy (central difference with dT=0.5 K) agrees with analytical estimates to within 0.1%. [uncertain]

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch01: Thermodynamics of Solids at High Pressure
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Ch01 introduces the Birch-Murnaghan equation of state and finite strain theory that form the cold-compression foundation of the SLB formulation. The pressure function P(f), BM3KT(f), and BM3GT(f) methods in MineralParams are direct implementations of Ch01 theory.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch02: Debye Model and Thermal Properties
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Ch02 covers the Debye function D3(x) and its use in computing internal energy, heat capacity, entropy, and free energy. The DebyeFunctionCalculator class implements all the Debye integrals that provide the thermal contributions in ThermoMineralParams.

Ch03 defines the Gruneisen parameter gamma and its volume dependence parameterized by q0. The SLB formulation extends this by expressing gamma(f), q(f), and eta_S(f) as explicit functions of finite strain using the mu(f) polynomial.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch04: Elastic Moduli and Seismic Velocities
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Ch04 introduces K_T, K_S, G, and seismic velocities. Ch05 provides the complete framework that computes all of these self-consistently. The formulas for K_T, K_S, and G with thermal corrections synthesize the individual topics of Ch01-Ch04 into one unified calculation.

The Gibbs free energy G computed by ThermoMineralParams is the input to phase equilibrium calculations (GibbsMinimizer, SolutionCalculator). The SLB formulation provides the free energies that are compared across phases and compositions to determine the stable assemblage.

SLB2011Solutions defines how endmember parameters are combined for solid solutions (e.g., olivine as a mix of forsterite and fayalite). The SLB per-endmember formulation of this chapter is the foundation on which mixing models are built.

RockCalculator and MixtureCalculator aggregate single-mineral SLB results using Voigt-Reuss-Hill or Hashin-Shtrikman bounds to predict rock properties along geotherms, directly comparing with seismological observations like PREM.

computational_methods:
The SLB2011 formulation is implemented across three primary classes. MineralParams stores the 11 fundamental parameters and provides algebraic methods: GetPressure(f) for the cold Birch-Murnaghan pressure, BM3Finite(P) for inverting P(f) via root-finding, BM3KT(f) for the cold isothermal bulk modulus, and BM3GT(f) for the cold shear modulus. Root-finding for BM3Finite uses a Regula Falsi optimizer with dynamically estimated bounds (fMax = max(0.02, 3*P/(3*K0) + 0.01)).

MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer implements the iterative self-consistency loop. Starting from the target pressure as an initial guess for the reference (cold) pressure, it: (1) solves for finite strain f via BM3Finite, (2) constructs a ThermoMineralParams to compute the full P(f,T), (3) adjusts the reference pressure by the residual (P_target - P_computed), and (4) repeats up to 500 iterations until |P_target - P_computed| < 10^-5 GPa. Convergence is typically achieved in 3-10 iterations. The optimizer returns convergence status, iteration count, and pressure residual for diagnostics.

ThermoMineralParams is the central computation class. Its constructor takes a converged (f, T, mineral) triple and computes all derived quantities in sequence: mu(f), gamma(f), eta_S(f), theta(f) = theta_0*sqrt(mu), Debye calculator, DeltaE from the Debye internal energy difference, thermal pressure DeltaP, and q(f). Properties K_T, K_S, G, alpha, Vp, Vs, Vb, F, and G are then available as lazily evaluated C# properties. Entropy is computed by numerical central difference: S = -(F(T+0.5) - F(T-0.5)) / 1.0 * 1000, constructing two additional ThermoMineralParams at slightly different temperatures.

The DebyeFunctionCalculator evaluates D3(x) by composite Simpson's rule with 500 subintervals, achieving double-precision accuracy. Asymptotic forms are used for x > 150 (D3 -> pi^4/(5x^3)) and x < 10^-10 (D3 -> 1). The internal energy, heat capacity, entropy, and free energy per atom are all computed from D3(x) and related expressions. Numerical stability is ensured by using e^(-x)/(1-e^(-x)) instead of 1/(e^x - 1) for large x arguments.
open_questions: How well does the quasi-harmonic Debye model perform above 3000 K where intrinsic anharmonicity and pre-melting effects become significant? Should an explicit anharmonic correction term be added to the SLB free energy?

The SLB2011 database assumes pressure-independent q0 in the thermal expansion of the strain polynomial, but high-pressure experiments suggest q varies with compression. How much does a pressure-dependent q affect predicted velocities in the lower mantle?
Can the 11-parameter framework be extended to simultaneously fit new data types such as phonon dispersion curves from inelastic X-ray scattering, or does this require a beyond-Debye model?

For iron-bearing minerals like bridgmanite and ferropericlase, the spin crossover of Fe2+ produces anomalous elastic behavior not captured by the basic SLB formulation. How should the SpinCrossoverCalculator be integrated with the SLB free energy to maintain self-consistency?

The Landau model for displacive transitions uses a tricritical exponent (1/4 for the order parameter). Is this appropriate for all transitions in the SLB database, or do some (e.g., the stishovite to post-stishovite CaCl2-type transition) require different critical exponents?
How sensitive are phase boundary predictions (e.g., the 660 km discontinuity) to uncertainties in the SLB parameters, particularly K'0 and gamma0 which are often the least well-constrained?
exercise_suggestions: Verify that ThermoMineralParams at f=0.0001 and T=300 K recovers the ambient reference properties (K0, G0, gamma0, theta0, V0) for forsterite to within 1%.

Plot the Gruneisen parameter gamma(f) for forsterite from f=0 to f=0.15 using the strain polynomial. Compare with the simple power-law approximation gamma = gamma_0 * (V/V0)^q0 and discuss when they diverge.
Compute the thermal pressure DeltaP as a function of temperature (300-3000 K) at fixed compression (f=0.05) for Mg-perovskite. At what temperature does the thermal pressure exceed 10 GPa?
Starting from the Helmholtz free energy F(V,T), derive the expression for K_T by computing K_T = V * d^2F/dV^2. Show that this is equivalent to the formula used in ThermoMineralParams.KT.

Compare the SLB2011 predictions for Vp and Vs along an adiabatic geotherm (1600 K potential temperature) with the PREM reference model from 0 to 800 km depth. Identify the major phase transitions and their velocity signatures.

Investigate the effect of eta_S0 on the shear modulus temperature derivative dG/dT. Compute G at 1000 K and 2000 K for forsterite with eta_S0 = 1.0, 2.3, and 3.5, and discuss the implications for seismic tomography interpretation.
Implement a test that verifies the Maxwell relation (dK_T/dT)_V = -(dP/dT)_V * (dK_T/dP)_T - V*(d^2P/dV^2)_T using numerical derivatives of ThermoMineralParams properties.

For the quartz alpha-beta transition (which uses Landau parameters Tc0, VD, SD), plot the order parameter Q, excess volume, and excess entropy as functions of temperature at P = 0 and P = 5 GPa. Verify that the transition temperature shifts with pressure as predicted by dTc/dP = VD/SD.

figure_suggestions.description: Schematic diagram of the SLB2011 calculation workflow: from 11 input parameters through the self-consistency loop to output properties
figure_suggestions.axes: Flowchart: MineralParams (11 parameters) -> MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer (P,T loop) -> ThermoMineralParams (all derived properties)
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show the three classes and their roles; highlight the iterative feedback loop between thermal pressure and finite strain; list all output properties (rho, K_T, K_S, G, Vp, Vs, alpha, F, G, S)

figure_suggestions.description: The three contributions to Helmholtz free energy (F0, F_cold, F_thermal) as functions of pressure at T = 2000 K for Mg-perovskite
figure_suggestions.axes: x-axis: Pressure (GPa, 0-140), y-axis: Free energy contribution (kJ/mol)
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show F_cold increasing parabolically with pressure; F_thermal as a large negative contribution that varies slowly with P; F0 as a constant offset; total F as their sum; label the relative magnitudes

figure_suggestions.description: Comparison of cold vs. thermal contributions to K_T and G for forsterite along a 1600 K adiabat
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot K_T_cold(f), K_T_thermal_correction, and K_T_total on same axes; similarly for G; show that cold contributions dominate but thermal corrections are 5-15% and systematically reduce both moduli relative to 300 K values

figure_suggestions.description: Strain dependence of the Gruneisen parameter gamma(f) and Debye temperature theta(f) for several mantle minerals
figure_suggestions.axes: x-axis: Finite strain f (0-0.15), y-axis: gamma (left axis, 0.5-2.0) and theta (right axis, 600-1400 K)
figure_suggestions.key_features: Include forsterite, bridgmanite, and ferropericlase; show gamma decreasing and theta increasing with compression; annotate the strain values corresponding to 410 km and 660 km discontinuity pressures

figure_suggestions.description: Convergence behavior of the MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer: pressure residual vs. iteration number
figure_suggestions.axes: x-axis: Iteration number (0-15), y-axis: |P_target - P_computed| (GPa, log scale)
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show several cases: low P (5 GPa), high P (100 GPa), and high T (3000 K); demonstrate that convergence is typically exponential and achieved within 5-10 iterations; mark the 10^-5 GPa tolerance

figure_suggestions.description: Complete property profiles for pyrolite composition along a mantle geotherm: density, K_S, G, Vp, Vs, and Vphi from 0 to 2890 km depth
figure_suggestions.axes: x-axis: Depth (km) or Pressure (GPa), y-axis: respective property units
figure_suggestions.key_features: Multi-panel figure showing all properties; overlay PREM for comparison; mark olivine->wadsleyite, wadsleyite->ringwoodite, and post-spinel transitions; demonstrate how the SLB formulation reproduces the major seismic discontinuities

historical_context:
The SLB formulation traces its intellectual lineage to two parallel developments in the mid-20th century. The first is the finite strain theory of Francis Birch (1947, 1952, 1978), who recognized that Eulerian strain provides a natural expansion variable for the elastic energy of compressed solids. Birch's equation of state, and its extension to elastic moduli as polynomials in finite strain, became the standard framework for analyzing static compression experiments. The second thread is the Mie-Gruneisen thermal model, developed through the work of Gruneisen (1926), Slater (1939), and Dugdale and MacDonald (1953), which relates thermal pressure to internal energy via the Gruneisen parameter.

The synthesis of these two approaches into a unified thermodynamic framework was achieved by Stixrude and Lithgow-Bertelloni in two landmark papers (2005, 2011). Their key contribution was expressing the Gruneisen parameter and Debye temperature as explicit functions of Eulerian finite strain, rather than as separate functions of volume. This choice ensures that all thermodynamic properties derived from the free energy are mutually consistent -- a property lacking in earlier parameterizations that treated elastic and thermal properties independently. The 2005 paper (SLB2005) laid out the theoretical framework and demonstrated its application to individual minerals, while the 2011 paper (SLB2011) provided a comprehensive database of endmember parameters for mantle minerals and extended the framework to include solid solutions and phase equilibria.

The practical impact of the SLB formulation has been enormous. The BurnMan open-source toolkit (Cottaar et al., 2014) made the SLB2011 database accessible to the broader geophysics community, enabling routine computation of mantle properties for comparison with seismological observations. The ThermoElasticCalculator project implements the same formulation in C# with a desktop GUI, making it accessible for teaching and research without requiring Python expertise. The SLB database has been updated and extended by several groups, and competing databases (e.g., Holland and Powell, 2011; Xu et al., 2008) use similar but not identical theoretical frameworks, creating a healthy scientific competition that drives improvements in both data and theory.
